frames.caption: Check balance bottom-up: for every node, compare the heights of its two subtrees. Math.Abs(l - r) > 1 anywhere means the whole tree is unbalanced.
frames.note: Balance is a property each node must satisfy, so we cannot just look at the root. The plan is one postorder pass: dive to the leaves, then on the way back up each node reports its height to its parent — and the moment any node is off-balance we stop early with the -1 sentinel.

frames.caption: Check node 1: left subtree height = 0, right subtree height = 0. (Leaf — both subtrees are empty.)
frames.note: A leaf has no children, so both subtree heights are 0 and |0 - 0| = 0 <= 1: trivially balanced. We return its height, 1 + max(0, 0) = 1. Recursion bottoms out here (null returns height 0); every check is built up from these base cases on the way back up the stack.

frames.caption: |0 - 0| = 0 ≤ 1 → node 1 is balanced. Return its height 1 + max(0, 0) = 1 to its parent.
frames.note: A leaf has no children, so both subtree heights are 0 and |0 - 0| = 0 <= 1: trivially balanced. We return its height, 1 + max(0, 0) = 1. Recursion bottoms out here (null returns height 0); every check is built up from these base cases on the way back up the stack.

frames.caption: Check node 4: left subtree height = 0, right subtree height = 0. (Leaf — both subtrees are empty.)
frames.note: A leaf has no children, so both subtree heights are 0 and |0 - 0| = 0 <= 1: trivially balanced. We return its height, 1 + max(0, 0) = 1. Recursion bottoms out here (null returns height 0); every check is built up from these base cases on the way back up the stack.

frames.caption: |0 - 0| = 0 ≤ 1 → node 4 is balanced. Return its height 1 + max(0, 0) = 1 to its parent.
frames.note: A leaf has no children, so both subtree heights are 0 and |0 - 0| = 0 <= 1: trivially balanced. We return its height, 1 + max(0, 0) = 1. Recursion bottoms out here (null returns height 0); every check is built up from these base cases on the way back up the stack.

frames.caption: Check node 3: left subtree height = 1, right subtree height = 1.
frames.note: Balance is a PER-NODE property, not just a root property. At this node we already have the two child heights from the recursive calls below; the node is locally balanced exactly when |height(left) - height(right)| <= 1. If it holds we hand the parent this node's height, 1 + max(left, right), and keep going.

frames.caption: |1 - 1| = 0 ≤ 1 → node 3 is balanced. Return its height 1 + max(1, 1) = 2 to its parent.
frames.note: Balance is a PER-NODE property, not just a root property. At this node we already have the two child heights from the recursive calls below; the node is locally balanced exactly when |height(left) - height(right)| <= 1. If it holds we hand the parent this node's height, 1 + max(left, right), and keep going.

frames.caption: Check node 9: left subtree height = 0, right subtree height = 0. (Leaf — both subtrees are empty.)
frames.note: A leaf has no children, so both subtree heights are 0 and |0 - 0| = 0 <= 1: trivially balanced. We return its height, 1 + max(0, 0) = 1. Recursion bottoms out here (null returns height 0); every check is built up from these base cases on the way back up the stack.

frames.caption: |0 - 0| = 0 ≤ 1 → node 9 is balanced. Return its height 1 + max(0, 0) = 1 to its parent.
frames.note: A leaf has no children, so both subtree heights are 0 and |0 - 0| = 0 <= 1: trivially balanced. We return its height, 1 + max(0, 0) = 1. Recursion bottoms out here (null returns height 0); every check is built up from these base cases on the way back up the stack.

frames.caption: Check node 8: left subtree height = 0, right subtree height = 1.
frames.note: Balance is a PER-NODE property, not just a root property. At this node we already have the two child heights from the recursive calls below; the node is locally balanced exactly when |height(left) - height(right)| <= 1. If it holds we hand the parent this node's height, 1 + max(left, right), and keep going.

frames.caption: |0 - 1| = 1 ≤ 1 → node 8 is balanced. Return its height 1 + max(0, 1) = 2 to its parent.
frames.note: Balance is a PER-NODE property, not just a root property. At this node we already have the two child heights from the recursive calls below; the node is locally balanced exactly when |height(left) - height(right)| <= 1. If it holds we hand the parent this node's height, 1 + max(left, right), and keep going.

frames.caption: Check node 5: left subtree height = 2, right subtree height = 2.
frames.note: Balance is a PER-NODE property, not just a root property. At this node we already have the two child heights from the recursive calls below; the node is locally balanced exactly when |height(left) - height(right)| <= 1. If it holds we hand the parent this node's height, 1 + max(left, right), and keep going.

frames.caption: |2 - 2| = 0 ≤ 1 → node 5 is balanced. Return its height 1 + max(2, 2) = 3 to its parent.
frames.note: Balance is a PER-NODE property, not just a root property. At this node we already have the two child heights from the recursive calls below; the node is locally balanced exactly when |height(left) - height(right)| <= 1. If it holds we hand the parent this node's height, 1 + max(left, right), and keep going.

frames.caption: Check(root) returned 3 (not -1), so every node satisfied |hL - hR| ≤ 1 → IsBalanced = true.
frames.note: Check(root) came back as a real height (3), not -1, which means no node anywhere violated the |hL - hR| <= 1 rule. So IsBalanced returns true. Each node was visited once and did O(1) work, giving the O(n) time and O(h) recursion-stack space the reviewer promises.
